content:
Numeric computing in high-level languages started with interpreted loops over scalars. **APL** (1960s) pioneered array-oriented programming — operations on entire arrays without explicit indexing — influencing later scientific stacks.

**MATLAB** (1980s) popularized matrix-first syntax for engineers. **Numeric Python** (1995, predecessor to NumPy) brought array objects to Python; **NumPy** (2006, consolidating Numeric/numarray) standardized the `ndarray`, ufuncs, and broadcasting rules used today.

As ML data pipelines grew from kilobyte tables to gigabyte feature matrices, vectorization became the default performance pattern: pandas, scikit-learn, and deep-learning frameworks all assume NumPy-style contiguous array math underneath. Modern ML prep is less about hand-tuning C loops and more about expressing transforms as vectorized array operations that compile to fast kernels.

timeline.label: Array languages
timeline.description: APL and successors treat arrays as first-class objects, inspiring whole-array semantics later adopted by NumPy.

timeline.label: NumPy consolidation
timeline.description: NumPy unifies Python array computing with ufuncs, broadcasting, and C/Fortran backends — the foundation of the SciPy stack.

timeline.label: ML pipeline scale
timeline.description: Large tabular and embedding datasets make vectorized prep mandatory; frameworks delegate numeric hot paths to NumPy/BLAS/GPU kernels.
